coredata.dc:title: Dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence detection of cholesterol and glucose based on carbon dots-cyanuric acid complex quenched by MnO2 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Qian
Gan, Lanlan
Zhu, Ying
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description:
The current fluorescence assays are generally restricted by their background fluorescence and scattering light, thus room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials show the promising prospect towards biosensing. Herein, a facile strategy has been proposed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence, which further achieved emitting phosphorescence in aqueous environment by cooperating with cyanuric acid (CA). Significantly, the dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence of CA-CDs could be obviously quenched by manganese dioxide nanosheets through F
o ¨
rster resonance energy transfer and dynamic quenching effect. On the basis of the generation of hydrogen peroxide by enzyme catalyzing the corresponding substrate, a universal sensing platform for detecting cholesterol and glucose was originally established. To be specific, the linear ranges were 0.02625∼2.1 mg mL−1 for cholesterol sensing, and 0.1∼20 mM for the detection of glucose, which exhibited general agreement with the physiological range, thus may broaden the avenue of assaying other targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Universal detecting-platform
Cholesterol
Glucose

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: RTP and FL quenching of CA-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CA-CDs
RTP and FL stability of CA-CDs
Characterization of MnO2 and the mechanism for MnO2 nanosheets quenching CA-CDs
Feasibility of CA-CDs/MnO2 as a sensing platform
Exploration of CA-CDs/MnO2 platform detecting cholesterol
Exploration of CA-CDs/MnO2 platform detecting glucose
Selectivity and interference of CA-CDs/MnO2 as a sensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( cstc2018jcyjAX0197 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of preparing fluorescent CDs and phosphorescent CA-CDs; (B) Designing the CA-CDs/MnO2-based platform to assay cholesterol and glucose.

Phosphorescent intensity of CA-CDs with various reaction time (A) and temperatures (B); RTP and FL intensity of CA-CDs with different reaction time (C) and temperatures (D); (E) RTP 3D counter of CA-CDs suspensions; (F) FL 3D diagram of CDs solution.
Photographs of CDs (A) and CDs/PVA (C) with sunlight and UV lamp on; Images of CA-CDs solution (B) and CA-CDs/PVA composite (D) under sunlight, UV lamp on and off.

(A) TEM images of MnO2 nanosheets; (B) Size distribution of MnO2 nanosheets by DLS; High-resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p (C) and Mn 3 s (D); (E) Normalized UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets, FL and RTP spectra for CA-CDs; (F) Phosphorescence spectra and corresponding images of CA-CDs (a), CA-CDs with MnO2 nanosheets for 3 mM (b) and 10 mM (c); (G) Phosphorescence spectra of CA-CDs with various concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets; (H) Plot of P 0/P-1 versus varied concentrations of MnO2 from 400 μM to 10 mM; (I) FL lifetime decay of CA-CDs without or with MnO2 nanosheets.

(A) RTP and (D) FL intensity of CA-CDs with diverse substances (a & g blank, b & h COD, c & i cholesterol, d & j COD with cholesterol, e & k MnO2, f & l MnO2+COD + cholesterol); (B) RTP and (E) FL emission spectra of CA-CDs/MnO2/COD with various concentrations of cholesterol; (C) RTP intensity at 485 nm and (F) FL intensity at 460 nm versus different concentrations of cholesterol.

(A) RTP and (D) FL intensity of CA-CDs with different substances (a & e blank, b & f GOx with Glu, c & g MnO2, d & h MnO2+GOx + Glu); (B) RTP and (E) FL spectra of CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx with various concentrations of glucose (RTP for 0.1-20 mM, FL for 0.08-20 mM); (C) RTP intensity at 485 nm and (F) FL intensity at 460 nm versus different concentrations of glucose.

Selectivity (A) and interference (B) of CA-CDs/MnO2/COD sensing cholesterol against amino acids and metal ions (10 mM); Selectivity (C) and interference (D) of CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx sensing glucose against other saccharides (20 mM) and metal ions (10 mM).

head.ce:title: Dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence detection of cholesterol and glucose based on carbon dots-cyanuric acid complex quenched by MnO2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, a strategy has been proposed to synthesis carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence, and their phosphorescence is further activated in aqueous environment through CDs cooperating with cyanuric acid (CA). Significantly, the dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence of CA-CDs could be effectively quenched by manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets through F
o ¨
rster resonance energy transfer and dynamic quenching effect. Based on the generation of hydrogen peroxide by enzyme catalyzing the corresponding substrate, a universal sensing platform for detecting cholesterol and glucose has been originally established, which also broadened the avenue of assaying other targets.

The current fluorescence assays are generally restricted by their background fluorescence and scattering light, thus room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials show the promising prospect towards biosensing. Herein, a facile strategy has been proposed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence, which further achieved emitting phosphorescence in aqueous environment by cooperating with cyanuric acid (CA). Significantly, the dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence of CA-CDs could be obviously quenched by manganese dioxide nanosheets through F
o ¨
rster resonance energy transfer and dynamic quenching effect. On the basis of the generation of hydrogen peroxide by enzyme catalyzing the corresponding substrate, a universal sensing platform for detecting cholesterol and glucose was originally established. To be specific, the linear ranges were 0.02625∼2.1 mg mL−1 for cholesterol sensing, and 0.1∼20 mM for the detection of glucose, which exhibited general agreement with the physiological range, thus may broaden the avenue of assaying other targets.

ce:section.ce:para: Benefited from their superior photostability, low toxicity and simple preparation process, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention in diverse fields of bioimaging [1,2], sensing [3–5], theranostics [6], chemical catalysis [7,8] and more. Therefore, tremendous efforts have been contributed to explore their fluorescence properties of CDs [1–8]. Although they are also valuable owing to attractive dual-emissive characteristic and long-wavelength emission, scarce investigations were performed to identify the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) feature of CDs. Basically, energy level structures (CO/CN/CN ) and narrow energy-gap between the singlet and triplet states serve as two indispensable factors for producing the RTP emission of CDs [9]. In this regard, one way was achieved by halogens, rare earth metals, aromatic carbonyl groups or deuterated carbon doping [10,11], while fixing CDs into a certain matrix such as layered double hydroxides [12], silicon dioxide [13], biuret/urea [14] and poly(vinyl alcohol) [15] through the hydrogen bonds or covalent fixation was considered as another method to obtain efficient RTP. However, the majority of CDs-based RTP material only exhibited the phosphorescence (Phos) emission in anhydrous conditions, since water molecule and dissolved oxygen may destroy the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, leading to the phosphorescence quenching of carbon dots solution. Thereby, preparing the RTP material originated from carbon dots in dispersion/aqueous solution encounters unavoidable difficulties, which restrict its applications especially for biosensing.

Emerging as one critical member of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets display various advantages including the broad absorption (200∼600 nm), specific surface area (SSA) and favorable ability of degrading pollutants [16–18]. Due to the wide-range overlapping between the absorption of MnO2 and the excitation or emission of CDs, MnO2 nanosheets could effectively function as the fluorescence (FL) or phosphorescence (Phos) quencher [19]. Usually, F
o ¨
rster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is proved to be the quenching mechanism, denoting the non-radiative energy transfer from an energy donor to an acceptor within around the distance of 10 nm [17]. Consequently, the material of MnO2 nanosheets is verified as a kind of favorable energy acceptor with the strong absorption capability while luminescent nanomaterials as donors for a FRET-based assaying system. Nevertheless, the current fluorescent sensing strategies based on CDs are blocked by their drawbacks of background fluorescence and scattering light, which may show the possible interference. In striking contrast, the phosphorescence-mediated assays could avoid the autofluorescence derived from complicated biological samples [20–23]. By virtue of the room temperature phosphorescence, the FRET-based sensing approaches with MnO2 nanosheets could improve the signal-to-noise ratio, thus achieving the more sensitive and specific detection.

Dual-channel luminescence probes (especially for fluorescence and phosphorescence) show the potentiality in the fields such as biosensing and bioimaging [24–26]. Usually, the FL/Phos dual-emissive probes are composed of one phosphor and another fluorophore rather than a single luminophore, thus exhibiting the complexity for the sensitive analysis. Meanwhile, the phosphorescent probes mainly include metal ions such as Ir3+ and Eu3+ [27], which restrict their applications due to the scarcity of metal resource and toxicity. Also, the current sensing strategies hardly achieve the universal sensing based on the specific interaction between the enzymes and their corresponding substrates. Therefore, it is valuable to develop the universal dual-signal FL/Phos probes with one component and metal-free towards the biosensing applications.

Hereby, we successfully prepared the CDs-based phosphorescence complex (CA-CDs), thus establishing a dual-channel sensing platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 to determine cholesterol and glucose (Fig. 1 ). Basically, methotrexate (MTX) served as the carbon source and was employed to synthesize blue fluorescent carbon dots via the hydrothermal method. By connecting with CA, the nanocomposite of CA-CDs formed and displayed both blue fluorescence and cyan room temperature phosphorescence in aqueous. Taking advantage of their favorable phosphorescence in presence of water, we applied the nanocomposite of CA-CDs along with MnO2 nanosheets to build an enzyme-substrate interaction sensing platform. Particularly, MnO2 nanosheets showed the reversible quenching effect towards both RTP and fluorescence of CA-CDs, and FRET as well as dynamic quenching effect were identified as the quenching mechanism. Subsequently, a certain enzyme and its corresponding substrate reacted to produce hydrogen peroxide, which reduced MnO2 to Mn2+ in acid conditions, thus facilitating the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets. Therefore, the phosphorescence and fluorescence of CA-CDs recovered, and quantificationally detecting the substrates with the dual channels was accomplished through this proposed sensing system. Additionally, the universal platform was employed to assay the typical examples of cholesterol and glucose with favorable sensitivity and selectivity.

Methotrexate (MTX, >99 %) was acquired from Nanjing Duly Biotech Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China), and cyanuric acid, MnCl2·4H2O, poly (vinyl alcohol) [PVA], l-lysine, l-tryptophan were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Similarly, tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMA·OH), l-threonine, cholesterol (CHOL) and cholesterol oxidase (COD) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) as quinine sulfate ehydrate from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Also, glucose (GLU), urea, NaOH, KCl, NaCl, MgSO4, l-valine, l-arginine, l-histidine, l-methionine, boracic acid, phosphoric acid, hydrogen peroxide, acetic acid, methanol, ethanol and isopropanol were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Again, glucose oxidase (GOX), 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES), maltose, α-Lactose, d-fractose, d-xylose, sucrose and mannose were acquired from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the commercial reagents were analytical grade and directly utilized with none further purification.

Initially, the steady-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Likewise, Tescan Mira 3 XH (Czech Republic) was utilized to obtain scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. Again, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) data were acquired by TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 kV, and we utilized ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) to obtain XPS surveys. Meanwhile, the size distributions were recorded by Zeta sizer nano dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, England). Again, the UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets was examined by Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). To identify the fluorescence lifetime of CA-CDs, a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England) was utilized. Besides, the phosphorescence lifetime of CA-CDs at excitation of 355 nm was obtained by HORIBA FL-3 spectrometer (Jobin Yvon) at ambient environment. Eventually, the lyophilization by PiloFD8-4.3 V (Charlotte, USA) was performed to acquire the CDs and CA-CDs powders.

Generally, we synthesized CDs through the hydrothermal carbonization of methotrexate. First, methotrexate (0.044 g) and ultrapure water (6 mL) were successively introduced to a Teﬂon-lined autoclave (25 mL), which was heated at 180 °C for 3.5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the crude product was centrifuged at 9600 rpm for 20 min and filtered with 0.22 μm of microporous membrane. Subsequently, we collected the supernatant and conducted the dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for 8 h to remove the unreacted substance. Then, CDs powders were obtained through the lyophilization. For preparing CA-CDs, 10 mg of CDs and 1 g of cyanuric acid were added into 11 mL of ultrapure water and slowly dripped with 1 mL of NaOH (0.5 g mL−1), and the mixture was subjected to the constant stirring for 12 h at 25 °C. Next, 6 h of dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) and freeze-drying were performed to collect the milk-white CA-CDs powders. Additionally, CA-CDs (0.08 g mL−1) were prepared during the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP and FL quenching of CA-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Specifically, the brown colloid MnO2 nanosheets (10 mM) were diluted to different concentrations (0.4, 0.6, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 mM) with UP water, and each concentration of MnO2 nanosheets (200 μL) was separately introduced with 500 μL of CA-CDs suspensions (80 mg/mL). Then, these mixtures were diluted by MES buffer (pH = 5) to the final volumes of 1200 μL, and all the solutions were incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min followed by the phosphorescence and fluorescence measurements.

During the process of assaying cholesterol by this universal sensing system, 500 μL of CA-CDs suspensions (80 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (7 mM) were added to 490 μL of MES buffer (pH = 6.7) and incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min. Next, 10 μL of parallel COD-solutions (50 U mL−1) and the ethanol solutions of cholesterol (100 μL) with diverse concentrations (26.25, 78.75, 105.00, 131.25, 157.50, 262.50, 525.00, 787.50, 1050.00, 1312.50, 1575.00, 1837.50, 2100.00, 2362.50, 2625.00 μg mL−1) were introduced to the nanocomposite of CA-CDs/MnO2, respectively. For the blank, 100 μL of ethanol replaced the cholesterol solution while other conditions remained unchanged. Accordingly, the mixtures above were incubated at 37 ℃ for 35 min, and their RTP and FL detections were performed at the excitation wavelengths (RTP:355 nm, FL:385 nm), while the excitation and emission slits were set at 20, 20 nm for RTP assay as well as 5, 10 nm for FL assay.

Primarily, 500 μL of CA-CDs (80 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (7 mM) were introduced into 340 μL of MES buffer (pH = 5) followed by incubating at 37 ℃ for 10 min. Then, 60 μL of parallel GOX solutions (70 U mL−1) and 100 μL of glucose solutions with various concentrations (0.08, 0.1, 0.4, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18 and 20 mM) were separately added into the previous CA-CDs/MnO2. Serving as the control, 100 μL of glucose was replaced by 100 μL of UP water and other conditions kept consistent. Next, these reactions were incubated at 37 ℃ for 35 min and subjected to the phosphorescence and fluorescence detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal phosphorescence, the synthesis of CDs was investigated with different reaction time and temperature. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the RTP intensity of CA-CDs gradually increased with the reaction time from 2.5 to 3.5 h and started to decrease after 3.5 h, describing that the carbonization time was associated with their RTP emission. Also, the reaction temperature increased from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃, revealing the optimized conditions of 180 ℃ that provided an appropriate carbonization degree for CDs (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CA-CDs displayed the similar variation tendency as the phosphorescent behavior with different reaction time and temperature of CDs (Fig. 2C and D). The RTP and FL excitation-emission maps for CA-CDs solution were explored. Accordingly, the optimal phosphorescence excitation and emission wavelength were 355 nm and 485 nm for CA-CDs, which exhibited the cyan RTP emission (Fig. 2E). Simultaneously, CA-CDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence emission at 460 nm while excited at 370 nm (Fig. 2F).

To directly observe the phenomenon, the digital images of CDs and CA-CDs (each for 10 mL) were recorded under the daylight and 365 nm of ultraviolet lamp turning on or off. As described in Fig. 3 A and B, the interaction between CDs and CA induced the cyan afterglow, which lasted for 4 s in aqueous phase. Besides, we wondered whether imbedding CDs in PVA matrix could also produce the same afterglow or not. Interestingly, only the blue fluorescence for CDs/PVA composite was observed, whereas CA-CDs/PVA exhibited both fluorescence and green phosphorescence (Fig. 3C and D). These findings demonstrated that the unique and stronger force formed between CDs and CA compared with that in CDs/PVA.

In addition, scanning electron microscope (SEM) was applied to observe their microstructure of CA-CDs, appearing as short and columnar crystal (Fig. S1A). Consistent with the SEM image, the size distribution of CA-CDs ranged from 712.4 to 1718 nm with the average size of 1162 nm, and both SEM image and XRD pattern of CA-CDs proved their crystal structure (Figs. S1B and S1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP and FL stability of CA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To broaden the applications of CA-CDs, their phosphorescence and fluorescence stability was investigated towards different ion strength, temperatures (T), pH and time. As shown in Fig. S2A, varied concentrations of NaCl scarcely influenced the RTP intensity. Nevertheless, the phosphorescence intensity gradually increased accompanied by the rising temperature from 30 to 45 ℃ and then decreased from 45 to 95 ℃ (Fig. S2B). Because T below 45 ℃ was beneficial to the formation of hydrogen bonds, and hydrogen bonds were inclined to be destroyed with increasing T (45–95 ℃). Again, varying pH values from acid to basic displayed around 1.5-fold increase for the RTP of CA-CDs solution (Fig. S2C), owing to the stronger CO⋯H hydrogen bonding in alkaline condition compared to the CO⋯H hydrogen-bonding with acid. When pH reached 12, CA gradually reacted with OH−, thus hydrogen-bonding was broken, resulting in RTP quenching. Besides, the RTP intensity remained stable with UV exposure up to 120 min (Fig. S2D). Regarding the fluorescence stability of CA-CDs, their FL intensity exhibited scarce variations with various ion strength and radiation time (Figs. S3A and S3B). In contrast, temperature obviously influenced the FL intensity of CA-CDs, and their fluorescence near linearly increased with the rising T from 40 to 75 ℃, because the broken hydrogen-bonding released CDs from CA-CDs (Fig. S3C). Also, pH values hardly affected the fluorescence of CA-CDs (Fig. S3D). These findings suggest that both RTP and FL of CA-CDs show the generally satisfactory stability towards various factors, indicating their broad prospect of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MnO2 and the mechanism for MnO2 nanosheets quenching CA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: With broad absorption spectrum and fast electron transfer, the material of MnO2 nanosheets has been widely employed as a fluorescence quencher based on the mechanism of FRET, which extends its promising value for biochemical sensing [16–18]. Here, MnO2 nanosheets also played the role as quenching agents. Initially, these nanosheets were systematically characterized by TEM, DLS, XPS and UV–vis spectroscopy. As described in Fig. 4 A, the obtained MnO2 nanosheets exhibited two-dimensional sheet-like structure, which provided a large surface for interacting with CA-CDs. Meanwhile, the average size of MnO2 nanosheets was measured to be 274 nm with DPI of 0.335 (Fig. 4B). As displayed in Fig. 4C, the high resolution of Mn 2p XPS showed two peaks of Mn 2p3/2 (642.0 eV) and Mn 2p1/2 (653.9 eV) [28,29]. To further identify the structure of MnO2 nanosheets, the peak of Mn 2p3/2 was fitted to six peaks, showing an agreement with the pyrolusite (MnO2) instead of manganite (MnOOH) [29]. Besides, Mn 3 s spectrum with △E of around 4.57 eV indicated that the valence state of Mn (IV) mainly existed for MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 4D) [17,28,29]. These results suggest the successful synthesis of MnO2 nanosheets. Next, the UV–vis absorption spectrum further confirmed the optical characteristic of MnO2 nanosheets, showing a peak at 360 nm and broad absorbance band from 250 to 600 nm (Fig. 4E). As indicated in Fig. 4E, the FL & RTP excitation and emission exhibited a large overlap region with the absorption of MnO2 nanosheets, thus MnO2 nanosheets could serve as the ideal energy acceptor through FRET. Meanwhile, CA-CDs could be applied as a superior energy donor by virtue of their acceptable RTP and FL stability. These findings provided the evidence for the possible occurrence of Phos and FL resonance energy transfer from CA-CDs to MnO2 nanosheets.

To investigate the quenching effect of CA-CDs solution by MnO2 nanosheets, their phosphorescent and fluorescent spectra with varied concentrations of MnO2 were acquired. As shown in Fig. 4F, the phosphorescence intensity of CA-CDs decreased accompanied by the increased amounts of MnO2, consistent with the images that cyan phosphorescence weakened in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 4Fa–Fc). In particular, the prepared MnO2 nanosheets showed the obvious RTP quenching effect of CA-CDs from 400 μM to 10 mM (Fig. 4G). Simultaneously, a linear relationship was built up between P 0/P-1 and different concentrations of MnO2 with the correlation coefficient of 0.986 (Fig. 4H). Meaningfully, their fluorescence peaks of CA-CDs also declined with the increasing concentrations of MnO2, while the blue fluorescence darkened (Fig. S4A). Likewise, the decreased fluorescence was also highly related with the concentration of MnO2 ranging from 600 μM to 10 mM, and the linear response was obtained for F 0/F-1 versus multiple concentrations of MnO2 with R2 of 0.989 (Figs. S4B and S4C). Consequently, the simultaneous quenching of phosphorescence and fluorescence for CA-CDs was observed when MnO2 nanosheets were added.

To elucidate the mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets quenching CA-CDs, the potential quenching effect was successively discussed in detail. Due to the obvious overlap between the absorption spectrum of MnO2 and the RTP and FL excitation spectra of CA-CDs, inner filter effect (IFE) and FRET may contribute to the Phos and FL quenching of CA-CDs. Accordingly, their fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime values of CA-CDs and CA-CDs with MnO2 were recorded and calculated. As shown in Fig. 4I, the fluorescence lifetime of CA-CDs decreased from 24.11 to 17.73 ns by adding MnO2 nanosheets (Table S1), and the related phosphorescence lifetime also decreased from 288.63 to 55.31 ms (Figs. S5A, S5B and Table S2). In this regard, the variations of lifetime decay were not in accord with the characteristic of IFE, where the lifetime of fluorophore keeps unchanged with quenchers. Thus, the current quenching of CA-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets was first identified as FRET, excluding the influence of IFE. Furthermore, an equation was introduced to evaluate the FRET efficiency in the following equation [30]:
(1)
E = 1 – τ a/τ 0
Where, E represents the energy transfer efficiency, τ 0 and τ a are FL (RTP) decay time of CA-CDs (donor) without or with MnO2 (acceptor), respectively. Hence, the energy transfer efficiency was calculated to be 26.46 % for the fluorescence quenching process, while the observed fluorescence-quenching efficiency was 70.98 % (Fig. S5C). These results suggest that FRET is partially responsible for the quenching effect, and other mechanisms should be considered.

For dynamic quenching effect (DQE), non-radiative deactivation by collision usually occurs between the excited-state fluorophore and the quencher [27]. As static quenching effect (SQE), a non-fluorescent complex forms for the fluorophore and quencher [31]. To address whether DQE or SQE played the role or not during the quenching process, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed to theoretically calculate the contribution of two effects:
(2)
I 0/I = 1+K SV[Q]
Where I 0 and I indicate the RTP/FL intensity of CA-CDs without and with MnO2, [Q] represents the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets. As exhibited in Figs. 4H and S4C, the linear relationships were established between I 0/I-1 of CA-CDs and the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets. Particularly, Stern-Volmer equation was plotted as F 0/F = 0.1953 CMnO2/ mM + 0.88 with R2 of 0.9890 for the fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4C), and the Stern-Volmer quenching constant of KSV(FL) =0.1953 mM−1. Similarly, P 0/P-1 versus the concentration of MnO2 was plotted as P 0/P = 0.2699 CMnO2/ mM + 0.994 (R2 = 0.986) for the phosphorescence quenching, obtaining the quenching constant of KSV(RTP) =0.2699 mM−1 (Fig. 4H). These findings demonstrate that FL and RTP quenching processes perfectly match the Stern-Volmer equation.

Basically, Stern-Volmer equation is applicable for both SQE and DQE processes, and the lifetime decay was further introduced to distinguish the two mechanisms. To be specific, the lifetime decay maintains unaltered for SQE, while the ratio of lifetime decay (τ 0/τ) is proportional to I 0/I for DQE [31]. As revealed in Fig. 4I, the fluorescent decay of CA-CDs (τ 0) was 24.11 ns, whereas τ decreased to 17.73 ns with the addition of MnO2. Therefore, the quenching mechanism was attributed to DQE instead of SQE. To further validate the dynamic quenching effect, Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) could be described in the following equation [31]:
(3)
K SV = k q × τ 0
where τ 0 denotes the FL or RTP lifetime decay of CA-CDs without MnO2 nanosheets as well as k q of the molecular quenching rate constant, and this equation is only suitable for DQE. In this work, KSV(FL) =0.1953 mM−1 with τ 0 of 24.11 ns, and k q(FL) was accordingly calculated to be 8.102 × 109 M−1 s−1 for the fluorescence quenching process, that was lower than the largest possible value of 1 × 1010 M−1 s−1 for the diffusion-controlled process of one molecule [31]. Likewise, KSV(RTP) =0.2699 mM−1 with τ 0 of 288.63 ms, and k q(RTP) was obtained as 9.35 × 102 M−1 s−1 for the RTP quenching process, also showing lower than 1 × 1010 M−1 s−1. Thereby, the results further confirm that DQE is responsible for the FL and RTP quenching process.

Overall, the quencher is generally required to diffuse to the fluorophore during non-radiative deactivation process of DQE, and the distance between donor and acceptor is less than 10 nm for FRET, and the two kinds of effects could coexist. Thus, FRET and DQE combine into a joint mechanism to regulate the phosphorescence and fluorescence of CA-CDs synchronously quenched by MnO2 nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of CA-CDs/MnO2 as a sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Apparently, their phosphorescence and fluorescence of CA-CDs suspensions could be dramatically quenched by MnO2 nanosheets. In addition, H2O2 could decompose MnO2 with weak acid conditions, thus the quenched signals could recover by introducing H2O2 and the reaction is as following:
(4) MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ = Mn2+ + 2H2O + O2 ↑
To be precise, MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to manganese ion (Ⅱ) and lost the quenching ability owing to the broken sheet-like structure. As a result, the quenched FL and RTP could gradually recover with increasing concentrations of H2O2, and H2O2 may serve as the media for CA-CDs/MnO2 assaying targets. Thus, we addressed whether CA-CDs/MnO2 could function as a sensing platform, because plenty of interactions between enzymes and substrates could produce H2O2 for biochemical reactions.

In this regard, two substrates of cholesterol and glucose were separately utilized to identify the feasibility of CA-CDs/MnO2 as a sensing platform. In brief, both cholesterol oxidase and glucose oxidase (enzymes) could produce various amounts of H2O2 in the presence of diverse-concentration substrates (cholesterol/glucose), inducing the RTP and FL signals to recover with different degrees. For cholesterol oxidase-based cholesterol analysis, the reaction is as following [32]:
(5) Cholesterol + O2 → H2O2 + 5-Cholesten-3-one → 4-Cholesten-3-one

Similarly, the reaction towards the oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase could be described as the following [33]:
(6)
d-Glucose + O2 +H2O → d-Gluconic acid + H2O2
Taken together, building up CA-CDs/MnO2-based RTP and FL dual-channel sensing platform could be theoretically achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of CA-CDs/MnO2 platform detecting cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol as well as its esters are fundamental components in human blood and closely associated with human health [34]. Long-term increasement of cholesterol in human serum may indirectly cause kinds of severe diseases including coronary heart disease, hypertension, arteriosclerosis and lipid metabolism dysfunction and more [35]. In human serum, the concentration of total cholesterol is normally limited from 1.3 to 2.6 mg/mL [36], thus it is of vital significance to develop rapid, reliable and selective strategies for assaying cholesterol within the scope of clinical diagnosis and therapy.

Initially, the principle of sensing cholesterol was presented in Fig. 5 A and D. Specifically, CA-CDs suspensions showed the relative-high phosphorescence and fluorescence intensity (curve a and g). After the addition of COD or cholesterol respectively, their RTP and FL signals remained invariable compared with that of CA-CDs only (curve b, c, h and i). Next, cholesterol was introduced to the mixture of CA-CDs and COD followed by incubation at 37 ℃ for 40 min, indicating little decline of RTP and FL signals (curve d and j). These phenomena demonstrated that COD, cholesterol or the mixture of COD with cholesterol producing H2O2 separately exhibited negligible influence to their RTP and FL of CA-CDs. When MnO2 nanosheets were introduced to CA-CDs, the phosphorescence and fluorescence were obviously quenched, and the cyan RTP and blue FL appeared darker than that of CA-CDs only (curve e and k, insets of Fig. 5A and D). In contrast, the quenched signals recovered with a large extent while cholesterol was added to CA-CDs/MnO2/COD due to the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets as Mn2+ (curve f and l). Also, the color of corresponding RTP and blue FL become brighter (insets of f, l in Fig. 5A and D). These studies provide the possibility of CA-CDs/MnO2 serving as the sensing platform for detecting cholesterol.

To acquire the optimal conditions for determining cholesterol by CA-CDs/MnO2, different incubation time of CA-CDs with MnO2, solvents of cholesterol, ratios of ethanol to water, concentrations of MnO2, volumes of COD and reaction time were tested. Primarily, CA-CDs were incubated with MnO2 nanosheets at 37 ℃ for 10 min, and the quenching effect showed the maximal, thus the incubation time of 10 min was applied for the following experiments (Fig. S6). Since cholesterol showed low-solubility in water, the solvents including ethanol, isopropanol and acetic acid were utilized to dissolve cholesterol and added to CA-CDs/MnO2/COD. As described in Fig. S7A, ethanol and isopropanol exhibited the obvious recovery of RTP and FL signals, and we chose ethanol owing to its acceptable toxicity. Howbeit, the high-concentration of ethanol may inhibit the enzyme activity, thus reducing the RTP and FL recoveries. To avoid this potential issue, the volume ratio of ethanol to water was optimized to be 1/12 with the total detection volume of 1300 μL (Fig. S7B). Again, different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets also affect the efficiency of RTP and FL quenching by FRET and DQE, further showing an impact on the recovery process. As shown in Fig. S7C, the optimal concentration of MnO2 was identified as 7 mM, which achieved the maximal recovery of signals. For the consumption of the enzyme, we optimized the volume of cholesterol oxidase to be 10 μL (Fig. S7D). Moreover, both RTP and FL intensity gradually increased along with the reaction time of cholesterol and COD in the sensing system of CA-CDs/MnO2, and reached the maximum at 35 min (Fig. S7E). Taken together, MnO2 nanosheets obviously quenched the RTP and FL signals of CA-CDs with the incubation time for 10 min, and then COD and cholesterol in ethanol were successively introduced to CA-CDs/MnO2 to react for 35 min at 37 ℃. Therefore, we achieved the optimal phosphorescent and fluorescent detections of cholesterol.

Taking advantage of the long-lived and visible RTP in aqueous media, we applied CA-CDs/MnO2/COD to detect cholesterol and explored the related linearity and detection limit. As revealed in Fig. 5B, the phosphorescence of CA-CDs/MnO2 gradually recovered with a linear relationship as the concentration of cholesterol increasing from 26.25 μg mL−1 to 2.10 mg mL−1, building the equation of P = 1.85 C CHOL/ μg mL-1 + 1576.39, R 2 = 0.981 (Fig. 5C). Also, the phosphorescence detection limit was determined to be 29.86 μg mL−1 according to the signal to noise ratio of 3. Again, the FL signal restored when the concentrations of CHOL increased from 26.25 μg mL−1 to 2.625 mg mL−1, and a linear relationship was plotted as F = 1.03 C CHOL/ μg mL-1 + 714.23 with R 2 of 0.991 (Fig. 5E and F), and the detection limit was 4.16 μg mL−1. Furthermore, the photographs for CA-CDs/MnO2 platform with various concentrations (26.25–2625 μg mL−1) of cholesterol were obtained to directly observe their RTP and FL behaviors. Briefly, the color of suspensions under sunlight varied from faint yellow to white with the rising concentrations of cholesterol, indicating the decomposition of MnO2 step by step (Fig. S8A). Consistent with the restoration of signals, the blue fluorescence and cyan phosphorescence became brighter and enhanced (Figs. S8B and S8C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of CA-CDs/MnO2 platform detecting glucose
ce:section.ce:para: As people around the world are suffering diabetes and the population of patients is still increasing, diabetes has been considered as one of the significant health issues for 21 st century [37]. Fundamentally, the abnormality of the blood-glucose level may cause multiple complications of retina, kidney, heart and neural system, which severely threatens human life [38]. Hence, continuous monitoring of glucose is indispensable part for the diagnosis and treatment of diabetes [38], and more researches should be devoted to develop the ways of sensing glucose.

Accordingly, we further employed the universal platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 to detect glucose. Primarily, the sensing mechanism of CA-CDs/MnO2 determining glucose was investigated (Fig. 6 A and D). Briefly, the prepared CA-CDs displayed the strong phosphorescent and fluorescent emissions (curve a and e). Subsequently, the mixture of glucose oxidase (GOx) and glucose was added to CA-CDs suspensions, and their RTP and FL nearly remained consistent with CA-CDs alone (curve b and f), suggesting that GOx, Glu and H2O2 hardly showed impact on both RTP and FL of CA-CDs. Next, the RTP and FL were quenched by MnO2 via FRET and DQE, and both colors changed to be dim (curve c, g and insets of Fig. 6A and D). When glucose was introduced to CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx, the phosphorescence and fluorescence signals restored as a result of the reduction reaction of MnO2 nanosheets and sharply reduced effect of FRET and DQE (curve d and h). Simultaneously, the RTP and FL color became brighter compared with that of CA-CDs/MnO2 (insets d, h of Fig. 6A and D). These findings suggest that this dual-channel sensing platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 could be employed to assay glucose.

To achieve the optimal glucose detection, the RTP and FL spectra were recorded with different volumes of glucose oxidase and reaction time, and the concentration of MnO2 was the same as that for the cholesterol detection (7 mM). Also, the optimum temperature for enzyme catalyzed reaction was 37 ℃, and the following experiments were performed at 37 ℃. As shown in Fig. S9A, the volumes of GOx increased from 0 to 100 μL, and the RTP and FL signals recovered to the largest degree with 60 μL of GOx. Additionally, the incubation time of the phosphorescence/fluorescence recovering was tested. When glucose was mixed with CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx, their RTP and FL signals progressively restored accompanied by the increased reaction time and arrived at a stable stage after 35 min (Fig. S9B).

Phosphorescent detections show the obvious advantages mainly including the elimination of the interference from the short-lifetime fluorescence background or scattered light for biological samples [39]. Thus, the linear relationships and detection limits of CA-CDs/MnO2 sensing glucose were studied through both RTP and FL. Precisely, the RTP intensity gradually recovered with the range of 0.1 mM–20 mM (Fig. 6B and C). A linear relationship was built up between the phosphorescence intensity and glucose concentrations (P = 120.65 C Glu / mM + 1572.81, R 2 = 0.988) with the limit detection of (LOD) 0.49 mM. Also, the fluorescence signals restored step by step with the increased glucose concentrations from 0.08 mM to 20 mM, which was applicable for the physiological range of glucose (Fig. 6E). Again, the FL variation of assaying glucose revealed a linear relationship (F = 83.19 C Glu / mM + 678.94, R 2 = 0.988) (Fig. 6F), and its LOD was calculated to be 0.48 mM. Furthermore, the images of CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx were obtained with various glucose concentrations (0.4−20 mM) in the absence and presence of UV lamp. Consistent with the previous linear relationship, the white color of the mixtures changed to be darkening with the gradually decreased concentrations of glucose under sunlight (Fig. S10A). With UV lamp on, the blue fluorescence became brighter by introducing glucose, and the cyan phosphorescence became brighter with UV lamp off (Figs. S10B and S10C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of CA-CDs/MnO2 as a sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity and potential interference of CA-CDs/MnO2 platform towards assaying cholesterol and glucose, a series of experiments were therewith performed. Evidently, their RTP and FL signals of CA-CDs/MnO2/COD with cholesterol displayed around 4-fold higher than the blank, whereas other substances such as amino acids and metal ions (L-tryptophane, l-methionine, l-threonine, l-lysine, l-histidine, l-arginine, l-valine, MgSO4, NaCl, KCl, urea, glucose, each for 10 mM) scarcely showed influence to the sensing platform (Fig. 7 A). Further, the anti-interference ability of this platform was studied, and the recovered RTP and FL exhibited a small fluctuation with an acceptable range in the presence of different interfering substances (Fig. 7B).

Similarly, this glucose sensing platform of CA-CDs/MnO2/GOx showed the high specificity (about 4.6-fold higher than that of the blank) against other saccharides and substances including sucrose, d-xylose, d-fructose, α-lactose, maltose (20 mM) or NaCl, KCl, urea (10 mM) (Fig. 7C). Again, this glucose sensing-platform indicated the satisfactory anti-interference property towards the possible interfering substances (Fig. 7D). Taken together, the proposed universal platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 showed the favorable selectivity and anti-interference ability, potentiating its applications for analyzing the targets of complicated biological samples.

To validate the practicability of CA-CDs/MnO2 for determining cholesterol and glucose towards the biological samples, the human serum without and with spiked were tested. As shown in Tables S3 and S4, the recoveries of cholesterol and glucose for the serum samples were between 85.79 % and 116.22 %, and their corresponding RSD (n = 3) for each concentration were less than 6.7 %. Besides, we have further compared the sensing platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 with the previous methods (Tables S5 and S6). On the whole, the phosphorescent assaying exhibited the superiority by avoiding the background interference and the current strategy achieved the dual-channel detection of cholesterol and glucose with the satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity.

Overall, the universal room temperature phosphorescence sensing platform was creatively designed and established based on the CD-based phosphorescent material. Basically, the CA-CDs proposed here simultaneously exhibited the fluorescence and phosphorescence in aqueous phase. Meanwhile, MnO2 nanosheets served as the ideal quencher and chemical oxidant. Interestingly, MnO2 nanosheets achieved both RTP and FL quenching of CA-CDs owing to the FRET and DQE. By virtue of the specific reaction between the enzyme and substrate, a RTP and FL sensing platform was successfully employed to detect cholesterol and glucose. Importantly, the sensing mechanism was elucidated as the destroyed MnO2 by H2O2, which produced by enzyme catalytic reaction, thus recovering their RTP and FL of CA-CDs. This universal sensing platform of CA-CDs/MnO2 broadens the avenues of determining other biochemical targets. The proposed study raises a way of preparing the water-resistant phosphorescent material and building a universal sensing platform, and the deeper effort may focus on the long-emission RTP materials towards the aqueous applications.
The data is available upon request.

Qian Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Lanlan Gan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ying Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Xiaoming Yang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2018jcyjAX0197).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red emissive two-photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular pH detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ye, Y. Xiang, Q. Wang, Z. Li, Z. Liu, A Red Emissive Two-photon Fluorescence Probe Based on Carbon Dots for Intracellular pH Detection, Small 15 (2019) e1901673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 5360-5363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of Iron (III) ions and dopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Qu, J. Wang, J. Ren, X. Qu, Carbon Dots Prepared by Hydrothermal Treatment of Dopamine as an Effective Fluorescent Sensing Platform for the Label-free Detection of Iron (III) Ions and Dopamine, Chem. 19 (2013) 7243-7249.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nano-sensors for pH and temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, Z. Xu, H. Cheng, H. Lin, M. G. Humphrey, C. Zhang, A Hydrothermal Route to Water-stable Luminescent Carbon Dots as Nano-sensors for pH and Temperature, Carbon 82 (2015) 87-95.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric detection of intracellular lysine and pH with one-pot synthesized dual emissive carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel optical biosensor for direct and selective determination of serotonin in serum by solid surface-room temperature phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Ramon-Marquez, A. L. Medina-Castillo, A. Fernandez-Gutierrez, J. F. Fernandez-Sanchez, A Novel Optical Biosensor for Direct and Selective Determination of Serotonin in Serum by Solid Surface-room Temperature Phosphorescence, Biosens. Bioelectron. 82 (2016) 217-223.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoming Yang respectively obtained his B.S. degree in 2004 and Ph. D. degree in 2009 at Sichuan University, China. During 2007–2009, he stayed at Purdue University as a Joint Ph.D. for pursuing his study in the field of biochemistry. Since 2010, he has been serving as a full professor in College of Pharmaceutical Sciences of Southwest University, China. His research interests mainly focus on the functional carbon dots, metal nanoclusters and their related applications as the fluorescent sensors.
